asm-assemble.name: Assembler (LLVM/clang)
asm-assemble.description: CONSTRUCT: assemble asm text (a file or --code) into machine-code bytes with the LLVM/clang integrated assembler — x64/x86/arm64/arm — emitted as hex, a C array, a raw blob, or JSON. Backed by clang (no fragile native python binding), so it works cross-arch anywhere clang does. The write-side partner of emulate-code: assemble a snippet, then run it on the virtual CPU. Transforms text→bytes; runs nothing.

prerequisites.install_hint: Install Python 3.8+ for the runner.
Install clang/LLVM (macOS: xcode-select --install; Linux: apt/dnf install clang).

check: command -v otool || command -v objcopy || command -v llvm-objcopy || command -v objdump
prerequisites.install_hint: Need a section extractor: otool (macOS Xcode CLT) or binutils/LLVM objcopy/objdump (Linux).

safety.notes: CONSTRUCT skill — assembles text into bytes with the LLVM assembler; it never executes anything. To run the bytes it produces, feed them to emulate-code (contained) or embed them in a stub via cc-build.

args.name: --code
args.desc: inline asm, e.g. 'mov rax, 1; ret' (';' splits statements)

args.name: --out
args.desc: write the raw bytes to this path

payload.note: BYO clang (+ a section extractor: otool on macOS, objcopy/objdump on Linux). Deliberately NOT keystone — keystone-engine ships no arm64-mac wheel, the same native-packaging trap as py7zr; the LLVM assembler cc-build already relies on covers x64/x86/arm64/arm cleanly. Closes rekit's shellcode loop: asm-assemble --out sc.bin → emulate-code sc.bin. `--format c` drops a ready-to-paste C array for a cc-build stub.

bin-triage.name: Binary Triage (format-agnostic)
bin-triage.description: Fast format-agnostic first look at any file, pure-stdlib: identify format from magic bytes (and route to the right analyzer), chunked Shannon entropy (packed/encrypted regions), string extraction with interesting-string surfacing (URLs/IPs/onion/shell/paths/exec-APIs), and an embedded-signature scan (mini-binwalk: ZIP/gzip/ELF/PDF at non-zero offsets). When given an output dir, also CARVES large embedded readable-source regions (the JS/text a single-file executable — Bun/Deno/pkg/nexe/SEA — appends after its native runtime) for rescanning. Emits BINARY.* atoms. Read-only — never executes the input.

prerequisites.install_hint: Install Python 3.8+ — pure stdlib, no other dependency.

safety.notes: Read-only. Reads bytes and computes magic/entropy/strings/signatures. Never parses as code or executes the input.

args.name: outdir
args.desc: if given, carve embedded readable-source regions here for rescanning

args.name: --max-bytes
args.desc: atom-analysis read cap (default 64 MiB; carving always scans the whole file)

payload.note: Zero-dependency first-look. Routes to the format-specific analyzers (pe/elf/macho/dotnet-analyze) or unpack. The embedded-signature scan is a lightweight mini-binwalk; use binwalk-carve for real extraction.

binwalk-carve.name: Firmware Carver (binwalk)
binwalk-carve.description: Carve and recursively extract embedded files from a firmware image or blob (filesystems, bootloaders, nested archives) with binwalk. The heavy sibling of bin-triage's embedded-signature preview. Prereq-gated on binwalk; honest blind spot with an install hint when absent.

prerequisites.install_hint: Install Python 3.8+ for the runner.
Install binwalk v3 (https://github.com/ReFirmLabs/binwalk) — a single Rust binary; some extractors also want unsquashfs/jefferson/sasquatch on PATH.

safety.notes: Trusted carver over untrusted firmware. binwalk scans signatures and runs extractors (decompress/unpack) — it does not run the firmware. Extractors are numerous; run this sandboxed (no network, writable output dir only) with the timeout.

args.name: outdir
args.desc: output dir for the carved tree

payload.note: BYO tool on PATH (binwalk pulls a whole extractor ecosystem; not bundled). Runs `binwalk -e` with cwd=outdir so the carved tree lands there on v2 or v3. Dispatcher prereq gate = honest degradation. Feeds the *-analyze / covert-scan skills on the revealed tree.

cc-build.name: C/C++ Compiler (clang)
cc-build.description: CONSTRUCT: compile C/C++/ObjC source into a native artifact (exe / .o / .s / LLVM IR) with clang (falls back to cc/gcc). For building PoCs, test harnesses, stubs, and shared libs rekit needs. Cross-compiles via --target/--arch. Compiles only — does NOT run the result (feed the output to exec-observe/emulate-code yourself). BYO C compiler.

prerequisites.install_hint: Install Python 3.8+ for the runner.

check: command -v clang || command -v cc || command -v gcc
prerequisites.install_hint: Install a C compiler: clang (macOS: xcode-select --install; Linux: apt/dnf install clang or LLVM) or gcc.

safety.notes: CONSTRUCT skill — produces a binary, never runs it. executes_input:no (the sample isn't executed). Caveat: compiling UNTRUSTED source still invokes a compiler (preprocessor/pragmas), a mild risk distinct from running a sample — hence tier 1 not 0. The typical input is your own PoC source (trusted). To run what it builds, hand the artifact to the dynamic tier explicitly.

args.name: --out
args.desc: output path (default derived from source + emit)

payload.note: BYO clang (or cc/gcc). Build a PoC and inspect it with a decompiler, or use --emit asm/ir to inspect compiler output. Pass shellcode or object output to emulate-code; pass executables to exec-observe only in an appropriate analysis environment.

dex-dump.name: Android DEX Dumper
dex-dump.description: Dump decrypted DEX and CompactDex images from a RUNNING Android app's memory with Rekit's clean-room Apache-2.0 device tool (ptrace on a rooted aarch64 device). Defeats class-loading packers by reading the image after the app decrypts it. Does not launch the app or execute a sample on the host. BYO rooted device + adb; build the device tool from the included Rust source.

prerequisites.install_hint: Install Python 3.8+ for the runner.
Install Android Platform Tools (adb): https://developer.android.com/tools/releases/platform-tools

safety.notes: Invasive but contained to a connected Android device — NOT host execution. Rekit's device tool ptrace-attaches to an app the operator already launched and reads decrypted DEX/CDEX from memory. The runner never launches the target. Requires a rooted aarch64 device, pushes the locally built tool to /data/local/tmp, pulls dumps, and cleans up. The target must be past its splash screen so its loader has decrypted the real image.

args.name: package
args.desc: target package name (e.g. com.example.app), or 'auto' to detect the foreground app

args.name: outdir
args.desc: local output directory for pulled DEX files

args.name: --device
args.desc: adb device serial (default: the single connected device)

args.name: --keep-tool
args.desc: leave rekit-dex-dumper on the device after pulling (default: clean up)

payload.note: Original clean-room, dependency-free Rust implementation. The build requires Rust 1.74+, the aarch64-linux-android target, and Android NDK r25+; it installs bin/dex-dumper. The runner executes it only on the connected device and pulls /data/local/tmp/rekit-dex-dump. No third-party dumper source or binary is included.

dotnet-analyze.name: .NET Assembly Analyzer
dotnet-analyze.description: Static triage of .NET / CLR managed assemblies (.dll/.exe) with dnfile: runtime version, assembly + referenced assemblies, type/method counts, IL-only vs mixed-mode, strong-name signing, unmanaged module refs, and the P/Invoke (DllImport) native-API surface classified by capability. Emits DOTNET.* atoms. Parses CLR metadata only — never loads or runs the assembly.

prerequisites.install_hint: Install Python 3.8+. dnfile is vendored (pure-python), so no network/install at analysis time. (No .NET runtime needed — this is static metadata parsing.)

safety.notes: Static CLR-metadata parse (dnfile). Never loads the assembly into a runtime, JITs, or executes it.

payload.note: Vendored via `uv pip install --target scripts/site` (pure-python, portable; NO .NET runtime needed). Complements pe-analyze: a native PE routes there, a managed PE is analysed here. The P/Invoke surface is where pure-IL malware touches the OS.

dotnet-decompile.name: .NET Decompiler
dotnet-decompile.description: Decompile a .NET / CLR assembly (IL) back to C# with the ilspycmd dotnet tool. Static: reads metadata + IL, never runs the assembly. Prereq-gated on ilspycmd (needs the .NET runtime); honest blind spot with an install hint when absent. Pair with dotnet-analyze for the P/Invoke surface.

prerequisites.install_hint: Install Python 3.8+ for the runner.
dotnet tool install -g ilspycmd  (needs the .NET SDK/runtime)

safety.notes: Trusted decompiler (ilspycmd) over an untrusted assembly. Reads IL/metadata and emits C#; never loads the assembly into a CLR or runs it. Timeout-bounded.

payload.note: BYO tool on PATH (needs the .NET runtime, not bundled). The runner invokes ilspycmd; the dispatcher prereq gate gives honest degradation. Complements dotnet-analyze (static triage → this for full C#).

elf-analyze.name: ELF Binary Analyzer
elf-analyze.description: Static triage of ELF binaries (Linux/BSD) with pyelftools: class/arch/type, sections + per-section entropy (packing), needed libraries, RPATH/RUNPATH, imported symbols classified by capability (network/exec/inject/load/crypto), and hardening (PIE/NX/RELRO). Emits BINARY.* atoms. Reads structure only — never runs the binary.

prerequisites.install_hint: Install Python 3.8+. pyelftools is vendored (pure-python), so no network/install at analysis time.

safety.notes: Static structural parse (pyelftools). Never loads, links, maps, or executes the binary.

payload.note: Installed via `uv pip install --target scripts/site` (pure Python, portable). Pair with hex-view for raw bytes; the analyzer emits structured BINARY.* atoms.

emulate-code.name: Code Emulator (Unicorn)
emulate-code.description: Emulate a raw code/shellcode blob on a virtual CPU (x86/x64/arm/arm64) with Unicorn and report the final register state, instruction count, and memory writes. Contained: the bytes run on an emulated CPU, not the host (memory-only; no host syscalls unless wired) — the safe way to 'run' shellcode or an isolated function.

prerequisites.install_hint: Install Python 3.8+. Unicorn is vendored under scripts/site.

safety.notes: Dynamic analysis, but CONTAINED — the code executes on an emulated CPU (Unicorn), never on the host. Memory-only by default (no syscall passthrough), instruction-count + timeout bounded. Because it's contained it is NOT behind the --allow-dynamic gate; it's the safe way to observe shellcode behavior.

payload.note: Vendored via `uv pip install --target` (native — platform-specific like the isolated-vm addon; ships wheels for common platforms). The dynamic tier's contained/emulation member: complements exec-observe (native run) and the tracer skills. Feed it shellcode carved by bin-triage or a function's bytes.

emulation-session.name: Stateful Emulation Session
emulation-session.description: Create and operate persistent Unicorn or Qiling emulation sessions across separate agent, CLI, or MCP calls. Use for iterative reverse engineering that requires stepping, register or memory changes, address/syscall/API hooks, traces, branch snapshots, or repeated inspection without restarting the emulated target. Contained execution only; never runs the target natively.

prerequisites.install_hint: Install Python 3.8+. Build the local runtime with `bin/rekit install emulation-session`.
Run `bin/rekit install emulation-session` with the same python3 Rekit uses. On macOS ARM64, git, cmake, and a C/C++ toolchain are also required.

safety.notes: CONTAINED dynamic analysis. Unicorn executes raw bytes in a memory-only virtual CPU. Qiling executes a binary against an emulated OS/rootfs. No native target execution. Calls are timeout/instruction bounded and serialize access to their session directory. Qiling can support passthrough when explicitly customized, but this runner does not enable it.

args.name: session
args.desc: caller-owned directory containing durable emulation state

args.name: --input
args.desc: raw code blob for Unicorn or full binary for Qiling

args.name: --rootfs
args.desc: matching BYO rootfs for a Qiling create

args.name: --argv
args.desc: JSON string array of emulated program arguments

args.name: --address
args.desc: address for execution, memory, mapping, or address-hook actions

args.name: --data
args.desc: hexadecimal bytes for memory write or search

args.name: --name
args.desc: register name, hook id, syscall/API name, or comma-separated register list

args.name: --value
args.desc: register value; unprefixed integers are decimal and 0x values are hexadecimal

args.name: --count
args.desc: instruction count or trace tail length

args.name: --timeout
args.desc: execution timeout in seconds, capped at 300

args.name: --perms
args.desc: memory permissions such as rw, rx, rwx, or numeric 1..7

args.name: --label
args.desc: human label for a mapping, hook, or snapshot

args.name: --snapshot
args.desc: snapshot id or unique label to restore

args.name: --stop
args.desc: make an address hook stop execution like a breakpoint

args.name: --trace
args.desc: enable instruction tracing when creating a session

payload.note: Original file-backed session implementation. The session directory is the persistence boundary: Unicorn regions/registers are serialized explicitly, while Qiling uses its documented snapshot/restore API. Qiling rootfs data remains BYO. Build explicitly because the combined runtime is large and platform-specific.

exec-observe.name: Execute & Observe (dynamic)
exec-observe.description: DYNAMIC: run a target in a fresh working directory with a timeout and capture its behavior — exit code, stdout/stderr, wall-clock time, and files it creates. EXECUTES the target; use the tracer skills for syscall, API, or network visibility. Run only where you accept the target's risk.

prerequisites.install_hint: Install Python 3.8+ — pure stdlib, no other dependency.

safety.notes: DYNAMIC — this RUNS the target, which can do anything the host allows (including network). Isolation is the operator's choice: run in a disposable VM or a dedicated analysis box, or bind an isolation provider. rekit's dispatcher gates this behind --allow-dynamic (consent, not a sandbox requirement); the runner also refuses direct calls without --yes-i-consent / REKIT_ALLOW_DYNAMIC=1. It does bound the run with a working-dir + timeout, but does NOT sandbox the target.

payload.note: The dispatcher requires --allow-dynamic and the runner verifies consent again. Consent is not isolation: use a disposable VM, dedicated analysis host, or another boundary appropriate for the target.

frida-api-trace.name: Signature-Aware Frida API Trace
frida-api-trace.description: DYNAMIC: spawn an authorized target under Frida and trace selected exported APIs using signatures parsed from a user-supplied API Monitor XML tree. Reports typed argument names, safe string previews, return values, hook coverage, and bounded events. The proprietary definition corpus is never shipped and stays gitignored. EXECUTES the target; consent-gated. BYO frida-tools and legally obtained definitions.

prerequisites.install_hint: Install Python 3.8+ for the runner.
pip install frida-tools (includes the Python frida bindings; use a matching frida-server for remote targets).

safety.notes: DYNAMIC — Frida spawns and runs the target natively. Consent-gated and timeout-bounded; the target may perform arbitrary host/network activity. Use an authorized disposable analysis environment. API Monitor definitions are local user-supplied data and are not redistributed.

args.name: --definitions
args.desc: API Monitor XML file/tree (default: ignored assets/apimonitor)

args.name: --apis
args.desc: comma-separated API names/globs; module!api is supported

args.name: --target-args
args.desc: command-line arguments passed to the spawned target

args.name: --timeout
args.desc: seconds before the spawned target is stopped (default 20)

args.name: --max-string
args.desc: maximum characters read from recognized string arguments (default 256)

payload.note: Rekit ships only the original parser/normalizer and generated Frida instrumentation. The API Monitor XML corpus is BYO, license-controlled user data stored in the gitignored assets/apimonitor directory or supplied with --definitions.

frida-android-instrument.name: Frida Android Instrumentation
frida-android-instrument.description: DYNAMIC: inspect an authorized Android app's Java runtime through Frida: list loaded classes, enumerate method signatures, or install observation-only hooks that report arguments and return values without intentionally changing them. Use for runtime discovery when static APK/DEX analysis is insufficient. Attaches to or spawns the app on a BYO Frida-capable USB device; executes the target and is consent-gated.

prerequisites.install_hint: Install Python 3.8+ for the runner.
pip install frida-tools, then provide a compatible frida-server or Gadget endpoint on the authorized Android device.

safety.notes: DYNAMIC — attaches to or spawns the Android app on a connected device. Consent-gated and timeout-bounded. Built-in hooks observe and call the original implementation; they do not intentionally alter arguments or returns. Instrumentation may still perturb or crash the app.

args.name: --class-name
args.desc: fully-qualified Java class for methods/hook mode

args.name: --method
args.desc: Java method name for hook mode

args.name: --match
args.desc: class substring or method glob, depending on mode

args.name: --spawn
args.desc: spawn the package instead of attaching to a running app

args.name: --max-events
args.desc: maximum retained classes, methods, hooks, and calls (default 200)

args.name: --max-value
args.desc: maximum characters retained for an argument or return preview (default 256)

payload.note: Rekit generates an original bounded Java agent and loads it through the BYO Frida CLI. No course scripts, APKs, Frida binaries, server, Gadget, or third-party payloads are redistributed.

frida-trace.name: Frida API Tracer
frida-trace.description: DYNAMIC: spawn a target under Frida and trace a curated set of network/exec/file/crypto API calls (dynamic instrumentation, no debugger). Logs the calls the sample makes. EXECUTES the target; consent-gated. BYO frida-trace (pip install frida-tools).

prerequisites.install_hint: Install Python 3.8+ for the runner.
pip install frida-tools (pulls the frida native core; may need matching device/host frida-server for remote targets).

safety.notes: DYNAMIC — Frida spawns and runs the target natively while hooking it. Consent-gated (--allow-dynamic; runner checks REKIT_ALLOW_DYNAMIC=1/--yes-i-consent). Timeout-bounded. Run in a disposable VM / analysis box; isolation is the operator's choice.

payload.note: BYO frida-tools on PATH. Complements syscall-trace (kernel view) with an API/library view; great for seeing which library calls a sample makes even when statically obfuscated. Default hooks are curated globs; override with --patterns.

ghidra-decompile.name: Ghidra Decompiler (headless)
ghidra-decompile.description: Decompile a native binary (ELF/PE/Mach-O) with full Ghidra headless analysis — higher fidelity than native-decompile (rizin pdg) for hard targets. Runs analyzeHeadless with a bundled Ghidra script that decompiles every function to one C file. Static: analyses + decompiles, never runs the binary. Prereq-gated on Ghidra's analyzeHeadless (needs a JRE 17+).

prerequisites.install_hint: Install Python 3.8+ for the runner.
Install Ghidra (https://ghidra-sre.org) + a JRE 17+; put <ghidra>/support/analyzeHeadless on PATH (or set GHIDRA_HOME and the runner will find it).

safety.notes: Trusted analyzer (Ghidra) over an untrusted binary — full auto-analysis + decompilation, never execution/emulation. Runs in a throwaway project dir (deleted after), analysis timeout-bounded. Ghidra's analysis is heavier than rizin's; use for hard targets.

payload.note: BYO Ghidra on PATH (a large JVM app; not bundled). We DO bundle the Ghidra post-script (scripts/ghidra_decompile.py, Jython) that decompiles all functions. The heavy sibling of native-decompile: native-decompile (rizin pdg) is Ghidra's decompiler without a JVM for the common case; this is full Ghidra for hard targets. Dispatcher prereq gate = honest degradation.

gitops.name: Git Operations
gitops.description: Drive git through plain verbs — clone, status, commit, branch, switch, stash, push, pull, remote-add, merge, cherry-pick, tag, worktree-add/list/remove, undo, discard, reset-hard — for harnesses or smaller models that can't or shouldn't hand-write git commands, refspecs, or worktree plumbing. Every op runs git non-interactively and returns ONE structured JSON result (branch, ahead/behind, staged files, new commit hash, ...), so the caller reasons about repo state without parsing git output. Data-loss ops (discard, reset-hard, worktree-remove, force push) refuse unless an explicit --force is passed. Use when an agent needs to commit/branch/stash/push or set up a worktree but you don't want it improvising raw git. Operates on your own trusted repo; never executes analysed samples.

prerequisites.install_hint: Install git (https://git-scm.com). macOS: xcode-select --install (or brew install git); Debian/Ubuntu: sudo apt install -y git.

safety.notes: Runs git against a repo you point it at (your own trusted tree, not a sample). Mutates repo state (commit/branch/stash/worktree); push/pull/fetch reach the remote. Data-loss ops refuse without --force; git runs non-interactively (no credential/editor/pager prompts) so it fails fast instead of hanging.

args.name: op
args.desc: which git verb to run (see SKILL.md for each op's args)

args.name: -C
args.desc: run inside this repo dir (default: cwd)

hex-view.name: Hex Viewer
hex-view.description: Non-interactive hex dump of a file or byte range (offset + hex + ASCII), with total size and sha256. Pure-Python stdlib, no dependencies. Reads bytes only — never executes the input.

prerequisites.install_hint: Install Python 3.8+ (standard on macOS/Linux). No other dependency — this skill is pure stdlib.

safety.notes: Read-only. Opens the file for bytes and prints a hex view. Never imports, parses as code, or executes the input.

args.name: --offset
args.desc: start byte offset (default 0; negative = from end)

args.name: --length
args.desc: bytes to show (default 256; 0 = whole file, capped at 1 MiB)

payload.note: Zero-dependency skill with a pure Python standard-library runner and no build step.

ioc-extract.name: IOC Extractor
ioc-extract.description: Pull indicators of compromise from a file or directory (URLs, IPs, domains, emails, md5/sha1/sha256 hashes, .onion, CVEs, BTC/ETH addresses, registry keys) — from text and from strings mined out of binaries. Every value is DEFANGED in output (hxxp://evil[.]com, 1[.]2[.]3[.]4) so a report can't ship a live IOC. Pure stdlib, read-only; never resolves or fetches anything.

prerequisites.install_hint: Install Python 3.8+ — pure stdlib, no other dependency.

safety.notes: Read-only. Regex over file bytes / extracted strings; defangs output. Never connects to, resolves, or validates any indicator.

payload.note: Zero-dependency. Domains are filtered against a filename-extension blocklist and IPs against benign/octet checks to cut noise; both raw `value` and `defanged` are returned. A reporting utility over any file or an extracted/decompiled tree.

intellidiff.name: Intelligent File and Folder Diff
intellidiff.description: Compare files exactly or with deliberate text normalization, compare directory trees, find duplicate files, calculate SHA-256 and CRC32 identities, and read bounded line ranges. Use for change analysis, duplicate/orphan discovery, checksum verification, or focused source inspection. Pure-stdlib and read-only; never executes input or follows symlinks.

prerequisites.install_hint: Install Python 3.8+; the runner uses only the standard library.

safety.notes: Read-only file and directory inspection. Streams hashes, decodes text only in smart/lines modes, writes nothing, and never follows symlinks or executes input.

args.name: --ignore-whitespace
args.desc: smart mode: ignore leading and trailing whitespace

args.name: --unicode-normalize
args.desc: smart mode: apply Unicode NFKC normalization

args.name: --binary
args.desc: include binary files in folder comparison

args.name: --include-hidden
args.desc: include hidden paths in tree operations

args.name: --context
args.desc: context lines before and after selection

payload.note: Adapted from the incoming IntelliDiff skill into one Rekit-contract entry point with structured output and SHA-256 identity checks.

ios-dump.name: iOS App Decryptor (砸壳)
ios-dump.description: Dump a decrypted iOS app binary (FairPlay 砸壳) from a jailbroken device via frida-ios-dump: attaches to the running app with Frida and writes the decrypted Mach-O from memory so it can be loaded into IDA/Ghidra/Hopper. BYO the frida-ios-dump toolchain + a jailbroken device with frida-server. Does NOT execute a sample on the host — attaches to an app the operator already launched on a connected device.

prerequisites.install_hint: Install Python 3.8+ for the runner.
pip install frida frida-tools (host must match the on-device frida-server major version)

safety.notes: Invasive but contained to a connected jailbroken iOS device — NOT host execution. frida-ios-dump attaches (Frida) to an already-running app the operator launched and reads decrypted memory; it SSHes to the device only to list/pull the dump. Requires a JAILBROKEN device with frida-server + SSH. No host sample detonation; not behind the --allow-dynamic gate. The app must stay in the foreground during the dump.

args.name: bundle_id
args.desc: target app Bundle ID (e.g. app.ish.iSH), or 'auto' to list running apps and pick

args.name: outdir
args.desc: local output directory for the dumped IPA

args.name: --host
args.desc: device IP (default: auto-detect via frida-ls-devices / usb)

payload.note: BYO toolchain (like dotnet-decompile BYO ilspycmd): clone frida-ios-dump, pip install -r requirements.txt, and `npx frida-compile dump.ts -o dist/dump.js` (the TypeScript agent must be built before dump.py runs). Point the runner at it via --dump-tool or FRIDA_IOS_DUMP. Prereq-gated on frida-ps; device must be jailbroken with a matching frida-server + SSH. Pair with the Mach-O analyzers on the decrypted binary.

js-covert-scan.name: JavaScript Covert-Tactics Scanner
js-covert-scan.description: Detect covert/evasive tactics in JavaScript/TypeScript: Unicode steganography (invisible/zero-width/bidi chars, confusable-homoglyph punctuation and letters), XOR/char-code/escape string hiding, and environment-keyed conditional behavior (timezone/locale/geo/proxy). Emits judgment-free parallax atoms (XFRM.UNICODE / XFRM.BITWISE / XFRM.ENCODE / LOAD.EVAL / ENVI.ENVCHECK), each tagged method="covert-scan" so the consumer's lens makes the obfuscation/evasion call by provenance, and flags when atom families co-occur. Static and read-only.

prerequisites.install_hint: Install Python 3.8+ — pure stdlib, no other dependency.

safety.notes: Read-only static scan (regex + Unicode category analysis). Never parses as code, imports, or executes the input.

args.name: input
args.desc: file OR directory of .js/.ts/.mjs/.cjs/.jsx/.tsx to scan

payload.note: Zero-dependency detector (Unicode via stdlib unicodedata + regex). Emits judgment-free parallax atoms (XFRM.UNICODE / XFRM.BITWISE / XFRM.ENCODE / LOAD.EVAL / ENVI.ENVCHECK), each tagged method="covert-scan"; the per-tactic note preserves which hiding tactic was seen, and the strongest signal is co-occurrence across families (hidden text + obfuscation + environment-keyed branching). Deliberately tactic-focused, not tied to any single incident.

js-deobfuscate.name: JavaScript Deobfuscator
js-deobfuscate.description: Deobfuscate and unpack obfuscated JavaScript (webcrack): reverses string arrays, folds constants, deflattens control flow, unminifies, and splits webpack/browserify bundles. Encoded string arrays are decoded by running the decoder inside an isolated-vm sandbox — not the whole program.

prerequisites.install_hint: Install Node.js >= 18 (an LTS is safest for the isolated-vm prebuilt ABI). The tool is vendored; no npm/network is needed at run time.

safety.notes: Primarily static AST transforms. To recover ENCODED string arrays it executes the obfuscated decoder function inside an isolated-vm sandbox (no fs, no network, memory/time capped) — a narrow slice, never the whole program. Run the node process itself sandboxed too: no network, writes only to the output dir, with a timeout.

args.name: outdir
args.desc: output dir; writes deobfuscated.js and, for bundles, unpacked/ modules

payload.native_dependency: isolated-vm (ships prebuilds for darwin-arm64/x64, linux-x64/arm64 glibc+musl, win32-x64)
payload.note: The local node_modules tree cannot be reduced to a single-file bundle because webcrack's isolated-vm native addon must load a platform binary. Build it from package-lock.json with rekit install js-deobfuscate.

js-sourcemap-extract.name: JS Source-Map Extractor
js-sourcemap-extract.description: Recover the original, un-minified sources from a JavaScript source map's sourcesContent — turning a bundled/minified .js back into a readable file tree. Accepts a .map (JSON) or a .js that references one via //# sourceMappingURL (inline data: map or sibling .map). Pure stdlib, read-only; path-traversal guarded (strips webpack:// and ../).

prerequisites.install_hint: Install Python 3.8+ — pure stdlib, no other dependency.

safety.notes: Read-only. Parses JSON (and base64-decodes inline maps) and writes sourcesContent to files. Never fetches a remote map, imports, or executes anything.

payload.note: Zero-dependency. Complements js-deobfuscate: if a map is present, this recovers the true original source instantly (no AST guessing); otherwise fall back to js-deobfuscate. Feeds js-covert-scan.

js-string-decode.name: JavaScript Constant-Key String Decoder
js-string-decode.description: Statically decode constant-key XOR / charCode string obfuscation in JavaScript so a downstream scanner can read the hidden strings (C2 URLs, victim domains, timezone names, shell commands). Finds decode sites with regex/heuristics — a `String.fromCharCode(x ^ KEY)` / `x ^ KEY` applied over a STATIC encoded literal (int array, string, or `\xNN`/`\uNNNN` blob, incl. a `Buffer.from(p,"base64")`/`atob(p)` front transform) — resolves KEY when it is a small int literal OR a variable assigned a small int nearby (best-effort constant propagation), applies the XOR, and writes the recovered plaintext to outdir/decoded-strings.js for rescanning. READ-ONLY: reads bytes and does arithmetic; never parses as code or executes the input.

prerequisites.install_hint: Install Python 3.8+ — pure stdlib (re, base64, mmap), no other dependency.

safety.notes: Read-only static decode (regex heuristics + XOR arithmetic + base64/escape decoding). Never parses the file as code, imports it, runs a JS engine, or executes any part of it. Complements js-deobfuscate (which runs the decoder in a sandbox) with a zero-execution path for the constant-key case.

args.name: outdir
args.desc: if given, write recovered plaintext to <outdir>/decoded-strings.js for rescanning

args.name: --format
args.desc: json | text (default json — the machine result the RE provider consumes)

args.name: --max-bytes
args.desc: cap on bytes read from the input (default 64 MiB)

fields.decoded: array of {key:int, count:int, sample:string} (grouped by key)

payload.note: Zero-dependency static decoder. Two-phase: (1) detect decoder FUNCTIONS (constant-key XOR-charCode body, optional base64/atob front transform) and decode their call-site payloads, resolving payload/key VARIABLES by nearest-preceding constant assignment; (2) inline sites (arr.map(c=>c^K), s.split('').map(...charCodeAt^K), for-of over an array/string/escape-blob). Emits decoded plaintext to <outdir>/decoded-strings.js as JS string literals so the fold rescans it as DerivedSource. Custom obfuscation is heuristic — see SKILL.md for the shapes it does and does not handle.

jvm-decompile.name: JVM / Android Decompiler
jvm-decompile.description: Decompile Java / Android bytecode (.apk / .dex / .jar / .class) back to Java source with jadx. Static: reads bytecode, never runs it. Prereq-gated on the jadx CLI (which needs a JRE); when jadx is absent the skill reports the honest blind spot with an install hint.

prerequisites.install_hint: Install Python 3.8+ for the runner.
Install jadx (https://github.com/skylot/jadx) and put it on PATH; jadx needs a JRE (java >= 11).

safety.notes: Trusted decompiler (jadx) over untrusted bytecode. jadx reads and reconstructs source; it does not run the input. Run sandboxed with a timeout; --no-res skips resource decoding.

payload.note: BYO tool on PATH (jadx is a large JVM app + needs a JRE, so it's not bundled). The runner just invokes jadx; the dispatcher's prereq gate provides honest degradation when it's missing. Feeds js/py covert-scan style review on the recovered .java.

macho-analyze.name: Mach-O Binary Analyzer
macho-analyze.description: Static triage of Mach-O binaries (macOS/iOS executables, dylibs, bundles, kexts) with macholib: arch/fat slices, file type, PIE, linked dylibs, RPATH, code-signature presence, and encryption (LC_ENCRYPTION_INFO). Emits BINARY.* atoms. Reads structure only — never runs the binary.

prerequisites.install_hint: Install Python 3.8+. macholib is vendored (pure-python), so no network/install at analysis time.

safety.notes: Static structural parse (macholib). Never loads, maps, or executes the binary.

payload.note: Installed via `uv pip install --target scripts/site` (pure Python, portable). Completes the PE/ELF/Mach-O triage trio and emits structured BINARY.* atoms.

minimal-executable.name: Minimal Executable Builder
minimal-executable.description: CONSTRUCT: generate a deterministic, conservative no-runtime executable that exits with a chosen status: Linux ELF (x86_64/i386), Windows PE (PE32+/PE32), or a static macOS arm64 Mach-O research artifact. Emits bytes, verifies its own header invariants, measures/hash-inspects the result, and labels proof honestly. Builds only; never runs the artifact.

prerequisites.install_hint: Install Python 3.8+ — the builder is pure stdlib.

safety.notes: CONSTRUCT skill — emits a fixed no-runtime exit stub inside conservative executable headers and validates the result; never runs it. Execute separately only in an environment appropriate for the artifact.

args.name: --arch
args.desc: x86_64 | i386 | arm64 (default x86_64; macho defaults arm64)

args.name: --exit-code
args.desc: exit status embedded in the artifact, 0..255 (default 0)

payload.note: One operational builder replaces the former format-specific tutorial skills. It emits conservative parser-clean shapes and keeps structural/tool-readable/loader/native proof claims separate. The static arm64 Mach-O is explicitly a research artifact, not a modern runnable claim.

native-disassemble.name: Native Disassembler
native-disassemble.description: Disassemble PE, ELF, or Mach-O binaries to function-oriented assembly without executing them. Automatically uses llvm-objdump/objdump, then falls back to Rizin/radare2 analysis for stripped binaries. Writes the complete listing to disassembly.txt and reports instruction/function-line counts.

prerequisites.install_hint: Install Python 3.8+ for the runner.

check: command -v llvm-objdump || command -v objdump || command -v rizin || command -v r2
prerequisites.install_hint: Install LLVM/binutils for llvm-objdump/objdump, or install rizin/radare2 as a fallback.

safety.notes: Trusted disassembler over an untrusted binary. Parses and decodes executable sections but never runs or emulates the target. Timeout-bounded; output is streamed atomically to disassembly.txt.

args.name: --tool
args.desc: auto | llvm-objdump | objdump | rizin | r2 (default auto)

payload.note: Pure-stdlib static wrapper with no vendored disassembler. Prefers the lightweight LLVM/GNU objdump path, then falls back to analyzed Rizin/radare2 function disassembly. No Ghidra or Java dependency.

native-decompile.name: Native Decompiler
native-decompile.description: Decompile a native binary (ELF/PE/Mach-O) to C-like pseudocode with rizin's built-in Ghidra decompiler (pdg over all functions). Static: analyses and decompiles, never runs the target. Prereq-gated on rizin/r2; honest blind spot with an install hint when absent. Pair with elf/pe/macho-analyze.

prerequisites.install_hint: Install Python 3.8+ for the runner.
Install rizin (https://rizin.re); its `pdg` command is a built-in Ghidra decompiler. (r2/radare2 also works.)

safety.notes: Trusted analyzer (rizin) over an untrusted binary — static analysis + decompilation, never execution/emulation. Timeout-bounded; scr.color disabled for clean text.

payload.note: BYO tool on PATH (rizin ships a large native toolchain; not bundled). Uses rizin's built-in Ghidra decompiler (`pdg`) — no separate Ghidra/JVM needed. Dispatcher prereq gate = honest degradation. Complements the *-analyze triage skills.

net-capture.name: Network Capture
net-capture.description: DYNAMIC: capture a target's network traffic to a pcap while it runs (tcpdump), then summarize packets, talking-to IPs, and DNS queries. Point --iface at a sinkholed interface for safer detonation. EXECUTES the target and captures on an interface (usually needs root); consent-gated.

prerequisites.install_hint: Install Python 3.8+ for the runner.
Install tcpdump. Capturing usually requires root (sudo) or capture capabilities.

safety.notes: DYNAMIC — runs the target natively and sniffs an interface (needs root). Consent-gated (--allow-dynamic; runner checks REKIT_ALLOW_DYNAMIC=1/--yes-i-consent). For safe detonation, run in an isolated network (a VM with a sinkholed/proxied interface) and point --iface at it. Duration-bounded.

payload.note: BYO tcpdump on PATH (root for capture). Writes a pcap + a talkers/DNS summary; feed the IPs/domains into ioc-extract. Pairs with syscall-trace for full behavioral coverage.

ord-lookup.name: Ordinal Import Resolver
ord-lookup.description: Resolve Windows DLL ordinal imports to exported symbol names from a vendored static database (ws2_32/wsock32, oleaut32). Stripped or by-ordinal imports become readable. Pure static lookup — reads no input file, runs no target.

prerequisites.install_hint: Install Python 3.8+ for the runner.

safety.notes: Pure static lookup against a vendored JSON database. Reads no sample, runs nothing. Aliases wsock32/mswsock → ws2_32. Use to enrich an import table dump where entries are ord#N rather than names.

args.name: dll
args.desc: DLL name (e.g. ws2_32, oleaut32.dll); omit with --list

args.name: --all
args.desc: dump every ordinal→name for the DLL

payload.note: The static database contains 515 ordinals across ws2_32 and oleaut32; wsock32/mswsock reuse the ws2_32 entries. The runner uses only the Python standard library.

pe-analyze.name: PE Binary Analyzer
pe-analyze.description: Static triage of Windows PE binaries (EXE/DLL) with pefile: machine/arch, subsystem, entry/imagebase, sections + per-section entropy (packing) and RWX flags, imports classified by capability (inject/exec/network/anti-debug/persist/crypto), exports, TLS callbacks, overlay, and Authenticode presence. Emits BINARY.* atoms. Reads structure only — never runs the binary.

prerequisites.install_hint: Install Python 3.8+. pefile is vendored (pure-python), so no network/install at analysis time.

safety.notes: Static structural parse (pefile). Never loads, maps, or executes the binary.

payload.note: Installed via `uv pip install --target scripts/site` (pure Python, portable). Pair with elf-analyze or hex-view; the analyzer emits structured BINARY.* atoms.

pe-unmap.name: PE Alignment Converter (pe_unmapper)
pe-unmap.description: Convert between PE file alignments via hasherezade's pe_unmapper (libpeconv): UNMAP a memory dump back to raw-file alignment, MAP raw→virtual, or REALIGN. Essential for finishing a memory dump into a loadable EXE. Windows-only vendored binary. Static: reads bytes, writes a converted file, runs no target.

prerequisites.install_hint: Install Python 3.8+ for the runner.

safety.notes: Static byte transform over a PE file — reads input, writes a converted output, never runs it. The vendored pe_unmapper.exe is a Windows PE32+ binary; run on a Windows analysis host (the runner errors clearly on non-Windows unless REKIT_ALLOW_WINE=1). BSD-2-Clause (pe_unmapper + libpeconv, hasherezade).

args.name: --base
args.desc: load base address in hex (e.g. 0x10000000); auto-detected if omitted

payload.note: Vendored Windows x64 release build (libpeconv-backed). The third step of dump recovery: OEP-find → dump → [this] realign → IAT-rebuild. Pair with pyscylla for the IAT rebuild step. Rebuild from source via scripts/build.ps1 (needs VS 2022 + CMake).

protection-survey.name: Source Protection Survey
protection-survey.description: Statically survey source trees for anti-analysis and protection patterns across C/C++, Objective-C, assembly, Rust, Go, Python, JavaScript/TypeScript, Java/Kotlin, C#, and Swift. Finds anti-debug/VM checks, runtime API resolution, executable-memory changes, early execution, custom sections, inline assembly, opaque/flattened control flow, stack strings, obfuscating build flags, injection primitives, and self-integrity checks. Emits evidence with confidence; read-only and never executes source.

prerequisites.install_hint: Install Python 3.8+ — the scanner is pure stdlib.

safety.notes: Read-only regex and co-occurrence scan over source text. It never imports, compiles, builds, or executes the input. Findings describe mechanisms, not intent.

args.name: --max-file-bytes
args.desc: skip source files larger than this size (default 2097152)

args.name: --max-findings
args.desc: maximum evidence records retained; counts remain complete (default 500)

args.name: --include-hidden
args.desc: include hidden files and directories except known generated/vendor trees

payload.note: Original explainable scanner with broad language/build-file coverage. It replaces the old unlicensed narrow survey script and reports review evidence rather than intent judgments.

py-covert-scan.name: Python Covert-Tactics Scanner
py-covert-scan.description: Detect covert/evasive tactics in Python: hidden code execution (exec/eval/compile of base64/hex/zlib/marshal output, pickle deserialization, dynamic import, chr()/\x string building), environment-keyed evasion (platform/VM/sandbox/analyst detection, timezone/locale, debugger/tracer checks), and Unicode steganography. Emits judgment-free parallax atoms (XFRM.UNICODE / XFRM.ENCODE / LOAD.EVAL / LOAD.DESER / LOAD.REFLECT / ENVI.ENVCHECK / ENVI.SANDBOX / ENVI.DEBUG), each tagged method="covert-scan" so the consumer's lens makes the obfuscation/evasion call by provenance, and flags family co-occurrence. Static and read-only.

prerequisites.install_hint: Install Python 3.8+ — pure stdlib, no other dependency.

safety.notes: Read-only static scan (regex + Unicode analysis). Never imports, compiles, or executes the input.

args.name: input
args.desc: file OR directory of .py to scan

payload.note: Zero-dependency detector, the Python sibling of js-covert-scan (same tactic model, emitting real parallax atoms XFRM.UNICODE / XFRM.ENCODE / LOAD.EVAL / LOAD.DESER / LOAD.REFLECT / ENVI.ENVCHECK / ENVI.SANDBOX / ENVI.DEBUG, each tagged method="covert-scan"). Strongest signal is decode-then-exec co-occurring with a sandbox/platform check (evasive dropper). Pairs with pyc-decompile for the Python-malware path.

pyc-decompile.name: Python Bytecode Decompiler
pyc-decompile.description: Decompile Python bytecode (.pyc / compiled code objects) back to source with decompyle3. Best for CPython 3.7-3.8 bytecode (e.g. PyInstaller-extracted payloads); reports honestly when the bytecode version is out of range. Static: reads bytecode, never runs it.

prerequisites.install_hint: Install Python 3.8+ to run the decompiler. The target .pyc can be a different version; decompyle3 targets 3.7-3.8 bytecode.

safety.notes: Static analysis: unmarshals the code object and reconstructs source from disassembled bytecode. Never imports, execs, or runs the .pyc.

payload.note: Vendored via `uv pip install --target scripts/site` (pure-Python → portable across OS/arch, unlike webcrack's native addon). decompyle3 targets CPython 3.7-3.8 bytecode; older bytecode uses uncompyle6, 3.9+ needs a version-matched decompiler — the runner reports the detected version and whether it's in range.

pyinstaller-extract.name: PyInstaller Extractor
pyinstaller-extract.description: Carve the Python out of a PyInstaller executable: locate the appended CArchive, extract every entry, and unpack the inner PYZ into individual .pyc files with reconstructed headers — ready for pyc-decompile. Pure stdlib. Static: parses archive structures and marshal.loads the PYZ TOC (deserialization, not execution); never runs the executable or the extracted code.

prerequisites.install_hint: Install Python 3.8+ — pure stdlib, no other dependency.

safety.notes: Static. Parses the CArchive/PYZ byte structures and marshal.loads the PYZ table of contents (deserialization — does NOT execute code objects). Never launches the executable or imports/runs any extracted module. Path-traversal + per-file guards on extraction.

args.name: input
args.desc: a PyInstaller-built executable (onefile or onedir main)

payload.note: Zero-dependency (pyinstxtractor-style). Closes the Python-malware chain: unpack → pyinstaller-extract → pyc-decompile → py-covert-scan. Reconstructs .pyc headers from the PYZ's embedded magic so decompyle3/pyc-decompile can read them (best on 3.7-3.8 targets).

pyscylla.name: IAT Rebuilder (libscylla bindings)
pyscylla.description: Dump, realign, and rebuild Import Address Tables of Windows PE binaries through Rekit's Python adapter for the external libscylla engine. Supports process enumeration, live dump, IAT search/parse/fix, XML tree round-trip, and reference scanning. Windows-only; bring your own arch-matched libscylla DLL and comply with its upstream GPL-3.0-only license. DYNAMIC on a live process (ReadProcessMemory) but does not execute the target.

prerequisites.install_hint: Install Python 3.8+ (3.10+ preferred). MUST match the target arch: x64 target → 64-bit Python, x86 target → 32-bit Python.

On Windows, obtain or build an arch-matched libscylla DLL from https://github.com/NtQuery/Scylla, review its GPL-3.0-only terms, and keep a private copy in skills/pyscylla/bin or set PYSCYLLA_DLL. Rekit does not download or commit it.

safety.notes: Reads a live process's memory (ReadProcessMemory / OpenProcess) to dump + rebuild its IAT — invasive but does NOT execute the target. Requires Windows, an operator-supplied libscylla DLL, and arch-matched Python: sizeof(DWORD_PTR) is baked into libscylla at compile time, so a 64-bit libscylla only correctly processes 64-bit targets (wrong arch = silently corrupt output). Use the `arch` op before dump/fix. Rekit's adapter is Apache-2.0; external Scylla/libscylla is GPL-3.0-only and remains the operator's licensing responsibility.

args.name: op
args.desc: version | arch | procs | dump | iat-find | fix | rebuild | tree | refs (see SKILL.md for each op's args)

args.name: --start
args.desc: iat-find search start address (default: image base)

payload.external: libscylla DLL supplied by the operator in the Git-ignored skills/pyscylla/bin directory, via PYSCYLLA_DLL, or on PATH
payload.license: Rekit adapter: Apache-2.0; external Scylla/libscylla: GPL-3.0-only
payload.note: Rekit does not commit, fetch, or build libscylla. A private local copy may live in the Git-ignored skills/pyscylla/bin directory. The operator must obtain it from upstream, comply with its license, and match the architectures of Python, libscylla, and the target. Dump-recovery path: iat-find → dump → fix → rebuild. Pair with pe-unmap for realignment.

qiling-emulate.name: Binary Emulator (Qiling)
qiling-emulate.description: Emulate a FULL binary (PE/ELF/Mach-O/…) with the Qiling framework: loads the program and emulates the OS/syscalls against a BYO rootfs, so a Linux ELF or Windows PE can be detonated on this host cross-arch/cross-OS WITHOUT native execution or a VM. Contained: syscalls hit Qiling's emulation, not the host kernel. Complements emulate-code (raw shellcode, no OS) and exec-observe (native run).

prerequisites.install_hint: Install Python 3.8+. Qiling is vendored under scripts/site (run `rekit install qiling-emulate`).

safety.notes: Dynamic analysis, but CONTAINED — the binary runs on Qiling's emulated CPU (Unicorn) and its syscalls are implemented by Qiling against the rootfs, not passed to the host kernel. File/network effects are emulated by default. Because it's contained it is NOT behind the --allow-dynamic gate (like emulate-code). NOTE: Qiling CAN be configured for syscall/network passthrough — that widens the blast radius and is the operator's explicit choice, not the default here. Wall-clock-timeout bounded via emu_stop.

args.name: --rootfs
args.desc: OS rootfs Qiling emulates against (BYO; must match target OS/arch)

args.name: --args
args.desc: argv passed to the emulated program

payload.note: BYO rootfs (github.com/qilingframework/rootfs) — matching the target's OS/arch backs the emulation. Qiling is heavier than Unicorn (pulls unicorn/capstone/keystone/pefile/…); vendored via `uv pip install --target`. The mid-tier between emulate-code (no OS) and exec-observe (native): full-binary behavior, contained, cross-platform.

r2-recon.name: radare2 Recon
r2-recon.description: Cross-format static recon of a native binary with radare2: drives r2 headless (-q -c, JSON commands) in one session for binary info, sections + per-section entropy, imports, exports, entry points, analysed functions, and decoded strings (incl. UTF-16). r2 auto-detects ELF/PE/Mach-O/DEX/..., so one skill covers them all and reports the relational view r2 is good at. Classifies imports by capability (inject/exec/network/anti-debug — Windows AND POSIX) and surfaces interesting strings. Emits BINARY.* atoms. Static: r2 analyses structure; it never runs or emulates the target.

prerequisites.install_hint: Install Python 3.8+ for the runner.

Install radare2 (https://radare.org) — `brew install radare2` (macOS), `apt install radare2` (Debian/Ubuntu), or build from source / `r2pm`. r2 is a large native toolchain, so it is BYO (not vendored).

safety.notes: Trusted analyzer (radare2) over an untrusted binary — static structural + analysis pass; r2 never runs or emulates the target. Timeout-bounded; scr.color disabled for clean, parseable JSON output.

args.name: input
args.desc: native binary — any format r2 detects (ELF/PE/Mach-O/DEX/...)

args.name: --analysis
args.desc: aaa (default, full) | aa (basic, faster) | none (structural only)

payload.note: BYO radare2 on PATH (a large native toolchain; not bundled). The cross-format, relational sibling of pe/elf/macho-analyze: those are pure-python structural parses for one format each; r2-recon is one skill over r2's auto-detect + analysed functions/strings/xrefs. Emits the same BINARY.* atoms so a caller can substitute freely. Complements native-decompile (rizin pdg) — recon here, decompile there. Dispatcher prereq gate = honest degradation.

secrets-scan.name: Secrets Scanner
secrets-scan.description: Find leaked credentials in files: high-signal provider patterns (AWS/GitHub/Slack/Google/Stripe/OpenAI/SendGrid/Twilio/JWT/PEM private keys) plus a generic high-entropy secret-assignment catch with placeholder filtering. Matches are REDACTED in output — never echoes a full secret. Pure stdlib, read-only.

prerequisites.install_hint: Install Python 3.8+ — pure stdlib, no other dependency.

safety.notes: Read-only. Reads text files and regex/entropy-matches. Redacts secret values in output. Never validates a key against a remote service, imports, or executes anything.

payload.note: Zero-dependency. Provider patterns are specific/high-confidence; the generic catch is entropy-gated + placeholder-filtered to limit noise. Values are redacted (prefix…suffix [len]); private keys report presence only. Emits SECRET.* atoms.

shellcode-stub.name: Shellcode Stub Wrapper
shellcode-stub.description: CONSTRUCT: wrap a raw shellcode blob into a runnable native PoC — a tiny C loader compiled with clang, whose exit code is the shellcode's return value. --os picks the loader: posix (mmap RW → memcpy → mprotect RX → call, default) or windows (VirtualAlloc RWX → memcpy → call → ExitProcess). --emit c dumps just the loader source. The last link in the write chain: asm-assemble → shellcode-stub → exec-observe (native) or qiling-emulate (cross-arch/cross-OS). Builds the exe; does NOT run it.

prerequisites.install_hint: Install Python 3.8+ for the runner.

safety.notes: CONSTRUCT skill — it BUILDS a PoC executable around the shellcode; it never runs it. The produced binary runs the shellcode when YOU run it — that's the dynamic tier (exec-observe, consent-gated) or a contained emulator (qiling-emulate). `--emit exe` needs clang (checked at runtime, honest error if absent); `--emit c` needs nothing but python3. `--os posix` (default) = mmap/mprotect loader for Linux/macOS; `--os windows` = VirtualAlloc+ExitProcess loader, default target x86_64-w64-mingw32, needs a mingw-w64 sysroot (runtime-checked — honest failure + install hint if absent). Both loaders' exit codes equal the shellcode's return value.

args.name: --hex
args.desc: shellcode as a hex string instead of a file

args.name: --emit
args.desc: exe (compiled PoC, needs clang) | c (loader source only)

args.name: --target
args.desc: cross-compile triple — needs a cross sysroot (see --cflags)

args.name: --cflags
args.desc: extra clang flags (e.g. --sysroot=… -fuse-ld=lld for cross builds)

payload.note: BYO clang for --emit exe (or --emit c and hand the source to cc-build). Completes the CONSTRUCT chain: asm-assemble --out sc.bin → shellcode-stub sc.bin --out poc → exec-observe/qiling-emulate. The loader exit code is the shellcode's return value, so a `mov w0,#42; ret` PoC exits 42 — easy to assert (verified end-to-end). `--os posix` (default) native host-arch build is the guaranteed path; a cross-OS --target needs a cross sysroot/toolchain (pass it via --cflags, e.g. --sysroot=… -fuse-ld=lld) — without one the link fails honestly. `--os windows` defaults to x86_64-w64-mingw32 and needs the mingw-w64 headers + sysroot (`brew install mingw-w64` on macOS) — the runner sniffs the failure and prints the install hint; cross-OS PoCs are then detonated under qiling-emulate with a Windows rootfs.

syscall-trace.name: Syscall Tracer
syscall-trace.description: DYNAMIC: run a target under a syscall tracer (strace on Linux, dtruss on macOS) and summarize its behaviour — files opened, network connects, processes exec'd, and a syscall histogram. EXECUTES the target; consent-gated. dtruss needs root + a permissive SIP.

prerequisites.install_hint: Install Python 3.8+ for the runner.
Install strace (Linux). On macOS dtruss ships but needs root + a permissive SIP.

safety.notes: DYNAMIC — the tracer runs the target natively; it can do anything the host allows. Consent-gated (--allow-dynamic; runner also checks REKIT_ALLOW_DYNAMIC=1/--yes-i-consent). Timeout-bounded. Isolation is the operator's choice — run in a disposable VM / analysis box.

payload.note: BYO tracer on PATH. The core behavioral dynamic skill; complements exec-observe (surface only) and emulate-code (contained). Feed the recovered files/network into ioc-extract for reporting.

unpack.name: Archive Unpacker (recursive, safe)
unpack.description: Recursively extract archives to a fixpoint: zip / tar(.gz/.bz2/.xz) / gz / bz2 / xz / asar (Electron) / ar / .deb with the pure-stdlib core, 7z and RAR via an external CLI (7z/7za/7zz, unar) when present. Walks the output for nested archives and extracts those too. Guards against zip-slip (path traversal) and decompression bombs (byte budget). Extraction is not execution — contents are written, never run.

prerequisites.install_hint: Install Python 3.8+ (3.12+ enables tarfile's safe 'data' filter). Pure stdlib for zip/tar/gz/bz2/xz.

safety.notes: Trusted extractor over untrusted bytes. Writes only inside the output dir; blocks path-traversal (zip-slip) and caps total/per-file output (decompression-bomb guard). 7z/RAR shell out to a trusted CLI over the untrusted archive. Never runs extracted content.

payload.note: Pure-stdlib core (zip/tar/gz/bz2/xz), no vendored deps. 7z/RAR route to 7z/7za/7zz/unar on PATH with honest degradation (py7zr was rejected: it pulls native, python-ABI-locked deps that break the portable-vendored model). Feeds pyc-decompile / the analyzers on the revealed tree.

yara-scan.name: YARA Scanner
yara-scan.description: Scan a file or directory with YARA rules (classic yara or the newer yara-x). Ships a small high-signal starter rule pack; point --rules at a real corpus (YARA-Rules, signature-base, your own) for serious coverage. Read-only — matches patterns, never runs the target. Prereq-gated on the yara CLI with honest degradation.

prerequisites.install_hint: Install Python 3.8+ for the runner.
Install yara (https://virustotal.github.io/yara/) or yara-x (https://virustotal.github.io/yara-x/) and put it on PATH.

safety.notes: Trusted matcher (yara) over untrusted bytes — pattern matching only, never execution. Timeout-bounded. (Rules can invoke modules; the bundled starter pack uses only strings/hex/regex.)

payload.note: BYO yara/yara-x CLI on PATH (native tool; not bundled). The starter pack at assets/starter.yar covers embedded/base64 PE, UPX, PHP webshell, Windows exec/download cradles, and curl|sh. Bring a full corpus via --rules.
